sail.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2022-23 value not reported in standardised table

sections.about: NOx emissions measure nitrogen oxides from high-temperature combustion, reheating, power generation, coke ovens, sinter/pellet operations and other thermal processes. This is disclosed under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator for air emissions other than GHG emissions. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Jindal Stainless led in FY2022-23 and FY2023-24 with 1.9807 kt and 1.7826 kt respectively, while SAIL led in FY2024-25 at 0.8 kt. Tata reduced NOx from 30 kt in FY2022-23 consolidated to 21 kt in FY2023-24, but increased to 24 kt in FY2024-25; JSW improved steadily from 28.7385 kt to 27 kt and then 25.845 kt. Jindal worsened in FY2024-25 to 2.5275 kt, but remained structurally below integrated BF-BOF peers due to scale and route differences. Tata’s FY2025 BRSR highlights real-time monitoring, upgraded pollution-control equipment, new technologies and maintenance as the core air-quality controls, while the BRSR capex disclosure explicitly includes investments in NOx reduction.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranked 3rd on NOx. Tata’s 24 kt was 23.2 kt higher than SAIL’s best-in-peer value of 0.8 kt, or approximately 2,900% above the best performer. Tata’s rank held at 3rd across FY2022-23, FY2023-24 and FY2024-25, although the absolute trend improved first and then worsened. The key issue is not relative rank deterioration, but the wide absolute gap to SAIL and Jindal in FY2024-25, indicating that route, operating boundary, scale and combustion intensity dominate the peer comparison.
sections.targets: Tata discloses investments and mitigation initiatives for NOx reduction.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel FY2022-23 is consolidated. SAIL FY2022-23 is not reported. Jindal Stainless BRSR reports NOx in MT; values are converted to kilotonnes.

flags.flagNote: SAIL FY2022-23 is not reported, so FY2023-24 trend cannot be assessed on a like-for-like numeric basis.
